description:
## Background

A local Beadwork ref move is not the only way an Issue List View can become stale. Ready is Beadwork-authored and time-sensitive: an Issue can enter Ready when its `defer_until` boundary passes without a new Beadwork commit. Also, a user returning to a backgrounded Beadsmith window expects the current Workspace's Issue List to be current immediately.

The core ref-driven coordinator and refresh failure handling establish one safe path for loading and publishing data. This slice adds time and focus triggers to that same path; it must not create parallel loaders or separate error behavior.

## What to build

Refresh the Current Workspace's Issue List once per minute while Beadsmith is active, so time-sensitive Ready membership updates without a ref change. Also refresh once when the window gains focus after the initial app load, so returning to the app catches up immediately.

Both triggers must be silent on success, coalesce with active ref-driven refreshes, preserve the Issue List through transient failures, and use the same workspace/revision admission rules as the core path.

## Technical Approach

- Add a 60-second time trigger and a focus-gain trigger to the existing refresh coordinator. Do not create another loader or another event protocol.
- Skip the first focus-gain event of an app session because the initial Issue List load already supplies the current snapshot.
- Send timer and focus requests through the same single-flight/dirty coordinator used for ref changes. If a load is active, coalesce rather than run concurrent Beadwork commands.
- Reuse the failure classifier, failure counters, banner behavior, workspace-selection generation, and refresh revision contract already established by prior slices.
- Keep the 60-second trigger unconditional. It is deliberately simpler and safer than inspecting deferred Issues to decide whether a time refresh is needed.

## Considerations

- The time trigger is responsible for Beadwork's time-derived Ready semantics; Beadsmith must not locally calculate readiness.
- A focus-gain event can coincide with a timer or ref-change trigger. The user only needs one newest snapshot, not multiple duplicate loads.
- The focus trigger must apply only on focus gain, never focus loss.
- The timer must stop or become inert when there is no Current Workspace.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] A deferred Issue whose `defer_until` boundary passes while Beadsmith remains open appears in Ready within 60 seconds without a Beadwork ref move.
- [ ] Regaining window focus after the initial load triggers an immediate refresh of the Current Workspace.
- [ ] The first focus-gain event of an app session does not cause a duplicate initial load.
- [ ] A timer, focus, and ref-change trigger arriving during one active load do not start concurrent loaders; the final rendered state is current.
- [ ] Successful timer and focus refreshes are silent and update the Issue List in place.
- [ ] Timer and focus failures use the existing transient/structural banner behavior and cannot overwrite another Workspace's state.
- [ ] No timer or focus refresh runs when no Current Workspace is set.

## Test Plan

- Unit-test the 60-second cadence with a fake clock and a controlled deferred Issue fixture.
- Unit-test focus-gain behavior, including suppression of the initial focus event and no action on focus loss.
- Unit-test coalescing when timer, focus, and ref-change requests race.
- Test refresh events retain the active workspace generation and newest refresh revision under all three triggers.
- Add a real desktop proof for focus refresh and an integration proof for a deferral becoming Ready without a ref move.
title: Refresh Ready on time and app focus
